title: Your first query against a real database
instruction:
You have access to a real company database with 5 tables:
- **employees** (id, name, department_id, job_title, salary, hire_date)
- **departments** (id, name, manager, budget, location)
- **projects** (id, name, department_id, status, budget, deadline)
- **customers** (id, name, email, country, joined_date)
- **orders** (id, customer_id, product, amount, order_date, status)

Query all departments to see what you're working with.
successMessage: You queried a real table with real data. The `ORDER BY budget DESC` sorted from largest to smallest. This database has 15 employees, 8 projects, 10 customers, and 18 orders — all ready to explore.

title: SELECT specific columns — don't fetch what you don't need
instruction:
Selecting `*` fetches every column. In production, that wastes bandwidth and exposes sensitive fields. Always name the columns you actually need.

Fetch just the employee names, titles, and salaries.
successMessage: 15 employees, sorted by salary. You'll notice VP/Head roles earn the most. In the next lesson you'll learn to filter — showing only engineering employees, or only those earning above a threshold.

title: COUNT — how big is the dataset?
instruction:
Before writing complex queries, it's useful to understand the scale of the data. `COUNT(*)` tells you exactly how many rows a query would return.

Count customers per country to see your customer distribution.
starter:
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM customers
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY customer_count DESC;
successMessage: 5 US customers, 2 Canadian, 3 international. `GROUP BY country` splits into groups, `COUNT(*)` counts each group. This pattern — group then aggregate — is the foundation of all reporting queries.
